data.name: Crispy Clam, Bacon, and Cheese Quesadillas
data.description: Smoky bacon and briny clams melt together with gooey Oaxaca cheese inside a perfectly blistered, golden-brown tortilla. Each bite delivers a deeply savory, crispy, and comforting coastal flavor profile perfect for a quick appetizer.

refrigerator.notes: Store in an airtight container lined with paper towels to absorb excess moisture.

freezer.notes: Freezing is not recommended as the clams will become rubbery and the tortillas soggy upon thawing.

storage.reheating: Reheat in a dry cast iron skillet over medium-low heat for 3 to 4 minutes per side until the exterior is re-crisped and the cheese is melted. Avoid the microwave, which will ruin the texture.

instructions.text: Drain the canned chopped clams through a fine mesh strainer. Press down gently to extract excess liquid, then pat the clams aggressively with paper towels until almost completely dry.
tips: Moisture is the enemy of a crispy quesadilla, so do not skip the patting step.

instructions.text: Place the diced bacon in a cold cast iron skillet, then turn the heat to medium. Cook until the bacon is crispy and the fat has rendered, about 5 to 7 minutes.

doneness_cues.visual: Bacon bits are deep golden brown and bubbling

instructions.text: Using a slotted spoon, remove the bacon to a paper towel-lined plate. Carefully wipe the excess bacon grease from the skillet, leaving just a thin film for flavor.
In a medium bowl, combine the shredded cheese, dried clams, crispy bacon, sliced scallions, and minced jalapeno. Toss well to distribute the ingredients evenly throughout the cheese.
Lay the flour tortillas flat on a clean surface. Distribute the clam and cheese mixture evenly over one half of each tortilla, then fold the empty half over the filling to create half-moon shapes.

Heat the skillet over medium heat, approximately 175 degrees C or 350 degrees F. Lightly brush the skillet with melted butter. Place two folded tortillas in the pan and sear until the bottom is golden brown and blistered.

doneness_cues.visual: Bottom of tortilla is golden brown and starting to crisp

instructions.text: Carefully flip the quesadillas with a spatula. Continue to sear until the second side is crisp and the cheese interior is completely melted and gooey.

doneness_cues.visual: Cheese oozing slightly from the edges, tortilla is completely toasted

instructions.text: Transfer the cooked quesadillas to a cutting board and let them rest for 1 to 2 minutes so the cheese sets slightly. Cut each half-moon into three wedges and serve immediately.

troubleshooting.symptom: Tortillas are soggy and fall apart when lifted
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Clams were not dried properly, leaking residual brine into the tortilla.
troubleshooting.prevention: Aggressively dry the chopped clams with multiple paper towels until they feel dry to the touch.
troubleshooting.fix: Return to the skillet over medium-low heat to evaporate excess moisture, though structural integrity may remain compromised.

troubleshooting.symptom: Tortilla burns before the cheese melts
troubleshooting.prevention: Maintain medium to medium-low heat so the filling has time to warm through gradually.
troubleshooting.fix: Move to a cooler zone of the griddle or lower the heat immediately. You can cover the pan briefly with a lid to trap heat and accelerate cheese melting.

chef_notes: Pre-mixing the filling ingredients ensures the shredded cheese acts as a structural glue, capturing the loose pieces of clam and bacon so they do not tumble out when flipping or eating.
Wiping out the skillet after cooking the bacon prevents the quesadillas from becoming excessively greasy. A very light brushing of fresh butter or oil yields the best, flakiest crust.
If you prefer to use fresh clams, steam little neck or cherrystone clams until they just open, chop the meat, and ensure it is fully cooled and patted dry before adding to the cheese mixture.
data.cultural_context: The quesadilla is a cornerstone of Mexican street food, originally consisting of simple local melting cheeses pressed inside a fresh corn tortilla. This appetizer embraces a fusion approach, merging the comforting format of a Mexican antojito with the classic Northeastern American flavor pairing of clams and smoky bacon. The result is a savory, ocean-kissed profile that playfully bridges coastal seafood traditions with weeknight accessibility.
